title: Greetings & Fillers
situation: You're meeting your friend's Indian roommate for the first time at a house party

phrases.context: 'arey' is a casual attention-getter, like 'hey' or 'oh'

phrases.hindi: sab badhiya? tum kya karte ho?
phrases.english: All good? What do you do?

phrases.hindi: accha accha, matlab tum yahan naye ho?
phrases.english: Oh I see, so you're new here?

phrases.hindi: haan yaar, chal milte hain phir
phrases.english: Yeah dude, let's catch up again

phrases.context: Universal reassurance phrase — use it everywhere

grammar_notes: 'arey' can express surprise, frustration, or just get attention depending on tone
'accha' alone means 'good' but repeated or with rising tone means 'oh really?'
'matlab' is a filler that means 'meaning/like/basically' — overuse it to sound natural
culture_notes: Indians often ask personal questions quickly (job, marriage) — it's friendly, not rude
'Tum' vs 'Aap': Use 'aap' for elders/strangers, 'tum' for friends/peers, 'tu' only for very close friends or when angry

skill_breakdown.explanation: Hindi conversations are full of fillers: 'arey' (hey/oh), 'accha' (I see/okay), 'matlab' (meaning/like), 'bas' (that's it), 'yaar' (dude). They make you sound natural.

more_examples.english: like, how do I say this...

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Tu' = very intimate/rude, 'Tum' = friendly/casual, 'Aap' = respectful/formal. Verb changes with each: tu karta hai, tum karte ho, aap karte hain.

practice_prompt: You just arrived at a friend's house party. You see someone new sitting on the couch. Start a casual conversation — introduce yourself, ask what they do, use fillers naturally.
